blocks.content: Spring came the way Georgia springs came — fast and sudden and warm enough to forget there'd been a winter. The hallways smelled different. People's moods lifted.

Ryan and Daisy were visible now in the way couples became visible in spring — walking together between classes, sitting close at the lunch table on the days he joined them. Nothing dramatic. Just the easy togetherness of two people who had stopped being careful about it.

Kit rode his bike home alone most afternoons now. Not because of anything said — Daisy still walked with him some mornings, still messaged on AIM, still had the same easy rhythm with him that she'd always had. But the afternoons were different. The afternoons had Ryan in them.
He didn't say anything about it. There was nothing to say.

One afternoon in April he was cutting through the parking lot behind the gym when he heard her laugh before he saw her. He knew the laugh — knew every version of it. This was the real one, the one that didn't always come out.
He came around the corner of the building and stopped.

Ryan was leaning against the wall. Daisy was facing him, close, one hand on his jacket. They were talking low, something private, and then Ryan said something and she laughed again and tilted her face up toward him, and Kit stood at the corner of the building and watched Ryan kiss her.
Not long. Not dramatic. Just the easy, familiar kind. The kind couples did when they were past being nervous about it.
Kit turned around and went the other way.
